perturbation.original_text: Each Party receiving Confidential Information under this Agreement shall maintain the information in confidence in accordance with the terms of this Agreement notwithstanding any termination of this Agreement for a period of three years from the date the Confidential Information is disclosed to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party receiving Confidential Information under this Agreement shall endeavor to maintain the information in confidence in accordance with the general spirit of this Agreement notwithstanding any termination of this Agreement for what it considers a reasonable period of time.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'endeavor to maintain' introduces ambiguity and reduces the obligation to a best effort basis. Replacing the fixed period of 'three years' with 'what it considers a reasonable period of time' is also ambiguous. This creates uncertainty around the duration of confidentiality, thus making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires actual or threatened misappropriation to be enjoined. By using 'endeavor' and 'reasonable period of time,' the contract weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, making it more difficult to prove actual or threatened misappropriation, as the terms are subjective and allow for varying interpretations of compliance. A fixed term provides clearer legal ground for enforcing confidentiality, which the original text has but the modified does not. The modified text's vague language undermines the legal protection afforded by the MUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA) by weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality. The original contract specified a three-year confidentiality period, whereas the changed text uses 'endeavor to maintain' and replaces the fixed period with 'what it considers a reasonable period of time.' According to the Law Explanation, this makes it more difficult to prove actual or threatened misappropriation due to the subjective nature of the terms. Scraped Law Snippet 1, MCL 445.1903, allows for injunctions against 'actual or threatened misappropriation'. By introducing ambiguity, the modified contract weakens the ability to obtain such an injunction, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall be identified prior to disclosure with an appropriate marking or identification such as CONFIDENTIAL or any similar legend.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information may be identified with a marking or identification, possibly CONFIDENTIAL, but such marking is not strictly required.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be identified' to 'may be identified' makes marking discretionary. Adding 'but such marking is not strictly required' further weakens the obligation. This ambiguity makes it difficult to prove what information was intended to be confidential, and increases the burden of proof in case of a dispute.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act, 'trade secret' means information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means by other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use, and is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. By making the identification of confidential information optional, the modified text undermines the 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, potentially jeopardizing its status as a trade secret under Michigan law. The original text requires all confidential information to be identified, thus satisfying the condition for efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy, which the modified text does not.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1902 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1902 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1902 Definitions. Sec. 2. As used in this act: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach, or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or any other means. (b) "Misappropriation" means either of the following: (i) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. (ii) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who did 1 or more of the following: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means an individual, corporation, partnership, association, governmental entity, or any other legal entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that is both of the following: (i) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. (ii) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History: 1998, Act 44
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The original text mandates identification of confidential information, supporting efforts to maintain secrecy as required by MCL 445.1902 (d)(ii). The changed text makes marking optional, undermining these 'reasonable efforts' and potentially jeopardizing the information's status as a trade secret under the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care that the Receiving Party uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will consider protecting the disclosed Confidential Information using a degree of care it deems adequate.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'will consider protecting' and 'deems adequate' introduce significant ambiguity. The original text sets a baseline of 'reasonable degree of care', while the modified version allows the receiving party to subjectively determine what is adequate, which might fall below the reasonable standard. This weakens the protection of confidential information and increases legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: According to the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act, a trade secret is subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Changing the obligation to use a 'reasonable degree of care' to using a degree of care the Receiving Party 'deems adequate' removes the objectivity and reasonableness standard, potentially failing to meet the required level of effort to maintain secrecy, which is a necessary condition to qualify for protection as a trade secret. The original text has the 'reasonable' standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1902 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1902 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1902 Definitions. Sec. 2. As used in this act: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach, or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or any other means. (b) "Misappropriation" means either of the following: (i) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. (ii) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who did 1 or more of the following: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means an individual, corporation, partnership, association, governmental entity, or any other legal entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that is both of the following: (i) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. (ii) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History: 1998, Act 44
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction. The original text requires a 'reasonable degree of care' to protect confidential information, aligning with the requirement in Michigan's Uniform Trade Secrets Act that trade secrets be 'the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy,' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 445.1902 (d)(ii). The changed text, however, states that the Receiving Party only needs to 'consider protecting' the information using a degree of care it 'deems adequate'. This subjective standard potentially falls below the legally required 'reasonable' standard, undermining the protection of trade secrets and creating a significant risk of non-compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall disclose any Confidential Information or other information received hereunder in any manner contrary to the laws and regulations of the United States of America or any applicable foreign export laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party will endeavor to avoid disclosing any Confidential Information in a manner that is knowingly against the general spirit of the laws and regulations of the United States of America or applicable foreign export laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not disclose' to 'will endeavor to avoid disclosing' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. Replacing 'contrary to the laws and regulations' with 'knowingly against the general spirit' creates a subjective standard that's difficult to enforce. This reduces the legal certainty and increases the risk of unintentional or intentional non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) require strict compliance with export control laws and prohibit unauthorized export of controlled technology. By changing the binding obligation to a 'general spirit' and 'endeavor to avoid', the agreement now permits a party to determine their obligations based on their understanding of the general intent of the law. This subjective standard contradicts the EAR's requirement for strict compliance with clear, defined regulations. The original text directly prohibits disclosing information contrary to the law, while the new text makes a subjective obligation based on an understanding of the general spirit.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not disclose' to 'will endeavor to avoid disclosing' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. The original text prohibits disclosing information contrary to the law, while the new text introduces a subjective obligation based on an understanding of the general spirit. This contradicts the need for strict compliance with export control laws, as the EAR requires compliance with clear, defined regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 CFR Part 732, covers steps for using the EAR, suggesting a need for following the regulations rather than interpreting their 'general spirit'. This undermines legal certainty and increases the risk of non-compliance. The modified language provides a loophole allowing the parties to not comply with export regulations which they are legally obligated to comply with.
